description: Lock art to pigment-backed Blombos evidence, keep pack transport separate from nautical navigation and traction yokes, anchor animal threshing to harnessed traction, and keep supercomputing on a sourced early-high-performance-computing chronology.

checks.reason: The launch-quality art node is anchored to the 73,000-year-old Blombos Cave ochre drawing rather than a Holocene placeholder.
The sourced first-known art evidence is ochre mark-making, so prepared pigment technology remains a direct enabling prerequisite.
Pigment creation enables the sourced art evidence without claiming that every form of art requires prepared pigment.
Natural dyes are scoped to textile dyeing and should not be the direct predecessor for early pigment-based visual art.
Rope and cordage are the direct load-securing predecessor for animals carrying packs.
Cordage enables load securing for pack animals without making rope the only possible material path.
The early_navigation node covers coastal and open-water navigation, not land pack-load transport.
Yokes and harnesses are traction equipment, while pack_animal_transport is scoped to animals carrying loads on their backs.
Animal-powered threshing uses harnessed traction or animal treading rather than plowing itself.
Harnesses and yokes enable animal traction for threshing but are not modeled as a strict universal hard requirement.
Heavy draft plowing is a related agricultural traction use but should not be the direct prerequisite for threshing sledges or treading.
The source-checked chronology anchors the node to IBM NORC as an early fastest-in-world scientific computer while noting label uncertainty for later Stretch, Atlas, and CDC 6600 histories.
The first-known supercomputing chronology predates integrated circuits, so ICs remain later implementation history rather than a direct prerequisite.
